1.synthesis: Chapter 31 is Job’s formal legal self-defense, the most comprehensive ethical self-accounting in the Old Testament. He opens with a moral covenant: “I have made a covenant with my eyes; how then could I gaze at a virgin?” (Job 31:1). The rhetorical framework is a series of conditional oaths: if I have done X, then let Y curse fall on me. Each oath invokes a self-curse as the guarantee of truth. His integrity rests on divine knowledge: “Does not he see my ways and number all my steps?” (Job 31:4). If he has walked with falsehood or his foot has hastened to deceit, let him be weighed in a just balance (Job 31:5–6). If his heart has been enticed toward another woman, if he has lain in wait at his neighbor’s door (Job 31:9), then let his wife grind for another and let others bow down over her (Job 31:10). He affirms that adultery would be a heinous crime, an iniquity before the judges, a fire burning to Abaddon that would burn all his increase to the root (Job 31:11–12).
1.historical_context: The covenant with the eyes (31:1) is remarkable for its inward orientation: Job does not merely abstain from adultery in act but makes a formal commitment not to let his eyes lust after another woman. This interiority anticipates the standard Jesus raises in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:28). The self-curse structure of chapter 31 draws on the ancient Near Eastern oath-formula in which a person invokes divine punishment on themselves if their statement is false—a form used in legal proceedings and treaty documents. The “let me be weighed in a just balance” (31:6) echoes the Egyptian Book of the Dead’s judgment scene where the soul is weighed against the feather of Ma’at; Israel demythologized this into the image of God as the just judge who knows the true weight of human character.
1.literary_structure: Chapter 31 is the most formally structured of Job’s speeches, using the conditional oath as its governing literary form throughout. Each section follows the pattern: “if I have done X (immoral act), then let Y (appropriate punishment) fall on me.” The oaths cover sexual fidelity (vv. 1–12), treatment of servants (vv. 13–23), avoidance of idolatry and greed (vv. 24–34), and concluding appeal to God (vv. 35–40). The comprehensiveness of the coverage—heart, eyes, hands, servants, strangers, enemies, land—makes chapter 31 the most complete portrait of integrated moral life in the Hebrew Bible.
1.theological_application: Job’s covenant with his eyes (31:1) and his conditional oaths covering thought as well as act demonstrate that the ethical standard he holds himself to is interior as well as external. He is not merely claiming behavioral compliance with social norms but moral purity at the level of desire and motivation. Communities shaped by this chapter are given a model of moral integrity that refuses to accept the division between inner life and outer conduct: the same God who sees his ways and numbers his steps (31:4) is witness to both the behavior and the intention behind it. Job’s innocence claim is therefore more radical than a claim about actions: it is a claim about the whole person as known to God.
1.christology: Job’s covenant with his eyes not to gaze with lust (31:1) is the specific ethical standard Jesus raises in the Sermon on the Mount: “everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart” (Matt 5:28). Job’s self-accounting anticipates the interior ethic Christ requires of his disciples. The request to “be weighed in a just balance” (31:6) is the posture of one who trusts God as the ultimate judge of interior reality—a trust that the gospel grounds: “it is the Lord who judges me. Therefore do not pronounce judgment before the time, before the Lord comes, who will bring to light the things now hidden in darkness and will disclose the purposes of the heart” (1 Cor 4:4–5).

13.synthesis: Job extends his moral self-accounting to his treatment of those in his power and those in need: “If I have rejected the cause of my manservant or my maidservant, when they brought a complaint against me, what then shall I do when God rises up? When he inquires, what shall I answer him?” (Job 31:13–14). The theological grounding is immediate: “Did not he who made me in the womb make him? And did not one fashion us in the womb?” (Job 31:15). “Have I withheld anything that the poor desired, or have I caused the eyes of the widow to fail?” (Job 31:16). Have I eaten his morsel alone, while the fatherless has not eaten of it? (Job 31:17). He has been father to the fatherless from his youth (Job 31:18). If he has seen anyone perishing for lack of clothing or a poor person with no covering (Job 31:19–20), and if he raised his hand against the fatherless because he saw his help at the gate (Job 31:21), let his shoulder blade fall from his shoulder and his arm be broken from its socket (Job 31:22).
13.historical_context: The theological reasoning in 31:15—“did not one fashion us in the womb?”—is among the earliest explicit statements of common human dignity grounded in shared divine creation. Slaves, servants, and masters share the same creator; the power differential between them does not negate the ontological equality of their creatureliness. This reasoning has no precise parallel in the ancient Near Eastern legal codes, which did not ground slave-rights in creation-theology. It anticipates Paul’s statement in Galatians 3:28: “there is neither slave nor free... you are all one in Christ Jesus.” The self-curse of the broken shoulder and arm (31:22) invokes a punishment proportional to the offense—the arm that oppressed the fatherless would be the arm that is destroyed.
13.literary_structure: The treatment-of-others section (vv. 13–23) moves from the personal relationship of master to servant (vv. 13–15) to the civic obligation to the poor, widow, fatherless, and stranger (vv. 16–23). The theological grounding in creation-equality (v. 15) appears in the middle of the first unit, giving it a foundational role: all subsequent treatment of the vulnerable derives from the truth that the same God made them both. The self-curse of the broken shoulder (v. 22) is the most physically specific of the chapter’s self-imprecations, suggesting that the hand raised against the fatherless is the body-part at stake.
13.theological_application: Job’s reasoning about servant-dignity—“did not one fashion us in the womb?” (31:15)—grounds social ethics in creation theology rather than in social convention or utility. Communities shaped by this verse have a theological basis for treating all human beings with dignity that transcends social hierarchy: not because all people are equally powerful, equally educated, or equally useful, but because all people share a common creator. Job’s refusal to dismiss his servant’s complaint (31:13) models the posture of the powerful person who takes the legitimate grievances of the powerless seriously rather than using social advantage to suppress them.
13.christology: Job’s grounding of human dignity in shared creatureliness—“did not one fashion us in the womb?” (31:15)—is deepened in the Incarnation to shared humanity: Christ himself is the one “born of woman” (Gal 4:4), the one who shares the human condition and therefore gives the fullest possible dignity to every human being. The obligation to the poor, widow, and fatherless that Job enumerates here is taken up by James as the definition of “religion that is pure and undefiled before God” (James 1:27): “to visit orphans and widows in their affliction.” Christ himself identifies with the hungry, thirsty, stranger, naked, sick, and imprisoned: “as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me” (Matt 25:40).

24.synthesis: Job extends his self-accounting to sins of the heart: “If I have made gold my trust or called fine gold my confidence; if I have rejoiced because my wealth was abundant or because my hand had found much” (Job 31:24–25). Or if he secretly worshipped the sun and moon—“if I have looked at the sun when it shone, or the moon moving in splendor, and my heart has been secretly enticed, and my mouth has kissed my hand” (Job 31:26–27)—this too would be a punishable iniquity, for it would be false to God above (Job 31:28). If he has rejoiced at the ruin of his enemy or exulted when evil overtook him (Job 31:29–30), or allowed his palate to sin by asking for a curse against him. If strangers were not satisfied with his flesh, and the traveler did not lodge in his court (Job 31:31–32). If he concealed his transgressions as others do, hiding iniquity in his heart because he feared the great multitude and the contempt of families terrified him (Job 31:33–34), so that he kept silence and did not go out of doors—then all the self-curses apply.
24.historical_context: The celestial worship charge of 31:26–27—gazing at sun or moon and blowing a kiss toward them—refers to a specific practice of ancient Near Eastern solar and lunar piety. Sun-worship was pervasive in Egypt and Mesopotamia and was a persistent temptation for Israel (Ezek 8:16; Deut 4:19). Job’s inclusion of this charge in his self-defense indicates that the prohibition applied broadly in the ancient world and that Job held himself to the standard against it. The concealment charge of 31:33—“if I have concealed my transgressions as others do”—may allude to Adam’s concealment after the Fall (Gen 3:8), positioning Job as one who has not followed the Adamic pattern of hiding from God when guilty.
24.literary_structure: The sins covered in this section are notably inner and subtle: trusting in wealth (31:24), secret celestial worship (31:26), rejoicing at an enemy’s ruin (31:29), concealing guilt from social pressure (31:33). These are not the gross social sins of oppression but the interior temptations that even an outwardly righteous person might harbor. Job claims innocence at this level too. The specific inclusion of inhospitality failure (the traveler who did not lodge in his court, 31:32) alongside the theological and psychological sins suggests that Job’s moral framework integrates private devotion, internal disposition, and concrete hospitality as components of a unified integrity.
24.theological_application: Job’s denial that he has concealed his transgressions out of fear of others’ opinions (31:33–34) names one of the most common forms of moral failure: the suppression of honest self-reckoning to preserve social standing. He refuses this move, choosing instead the far more dangerous path of public oath-taking about his innocence. Communities shaped by Job’s moral accounting are given a standard that includes the interior level of wealth-trust, enemy-rejoicing, and hidden guilt alongside the external actions. The model is integrity all the way down: no level of private life is exempt from the same ethical examination that Job applies to his public conduct.
24.christology: Job’s denial that he has trusted in gold or rejoiced in his wealth (31:24) is the posture Christ demands of his disciples: “you cannot serve God and money” (Matt 6:24). The rejection of celestial worship (31:26–27) is fulfilled in Paul’s declaration that Christ is above all heavenly powers: “he is the head of all rule and authority” (Col 2:10)—the sun and moon that Job refused to worship are themselves creatures subject to the one Christ. Job’s refusal to conceal transgressions from God out of social fear (31:33) models the posture of those who fear God more than people: “we must obey God rather than men” (Acts 5:29).

35.synthesis: Job closes his self-defense with the most dramatic legal demand in the book: “Oh, that I had one to hear me! (Here is my signature! Let the Almighty answer me!) Oh, that I had the indictment written by my adversary!” (Job 31:35). If he had such an indictment, he would carry it on his shoulder and bind it on him as a crown (Job 31:36). “I would give him an account of all my steps; like a prince I would approach him” (Job 31:37). He adds brief oaths about his land: if his land has cried out against him, if he has eaten its yield without payment, if he has caused the death of its owners, then let thorns grow instead of wheat and weeds instead of barley (Job 31:38–40). “The words of Job are ended” (Job 31:40). This closing statement—both his signature on the case and his invitation for a divine counter-indictment—is the legal culmination of everything Job has argued since chapter 3. He will approach God not as a penitent but as a prince: confident in his case, demanding a hearing.
35.historical_context: Job’s demand for a written indictment from his adversary (31:35) draws on ancient Near Eastern legal procedure where formal charges were written and presented to the defendant. In ancient Mesopotamian and Egyptian legal contexts, a written indictment was the defendant’s opportunity to know precisely what he was accused of and to prepare a counter-argument. Job’s insistence that he would wear the indictment as a crown inverts the normal shame of being accused: he would display the charges publicly as a badge of his willingness to be examined. The image of approaching God “like a prince” (31:37) contrasts with the normal posture of the creature before the creator but is the posture of one who has done nothing wrong and knows it. The closing formula “the words of Job are ended” marks a literary boundary between the dialogue-and-monologue section of the book and the Elihu speeches and divine answer that follow.
35.literary_structure: The final appeal of vv. 35–40 is the legal and rhetorical climax of the entire first half of the book. Job’s “signature” (v. 35) is the final attestation of his oath of innocence: he has put his name to the whole self-accounting of chapter 31 and the entire record of his complaint from chapter 3 onward. The invitation for a divine counter-indictment is the boldest moment of the book: Job is inviting God to prosecute him if there is a case. The wearing of the indictment as a crown (v. 36) is the posture of one so confident in his innocence that public examination holds no terror. The divine speeches that follow are God’s answer to this invitation—not a counter-indictment but a cross-examination about the nature of reality.
35.theological_application: Job’s “here is my signature” (31:35) and the willingness to approach God “like a prince” with a full account of all his steps (31:37) model a form of confidence before God that is grounded not in arrogance but in integrity. He is not claiming to have no need of God’s mercy; he is claiming that in the specific matter of his suffering, he has not done what the friends accused him of. Communities shaped by Job are given permission to approach God with confidence in specific matters of conscience: the same God who sees all steps and weighs in a just balance is the one to whom the appeal can be made. The willingness to be fully examined by God is itself a form of prayer that the book commends.
35.christology: Job’s demand for a divine hearing and his willingness to approach God “like a prince” with a full account of all his steps (31:37) is fulfilled in Christ, who gives his people bold access to the throne of grace: “Let us therefore come boldly to the throne of grace, that we may obtain mercy and find grace to help in time of need” (Heb 4:16). The confidence Job claims on the basis of his own righteousness is the confidence the believer claims on the basis of Christ’s: “since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God, let us hold fast our confession” (Heb 4:14). Job’s “words are ended” (31:40)—the human argument has reached its limit; the divine answer in the whirlwind must now speak.
